* **TOP NEWS**

**" Ghislaine Maxwell transferred to prison camp in Texas," **via AP**** -- "**Jeffrey Epstein 's** former girlfriend, **Ghislaine Maxwell** , has been moved from a federal prison in Florida to a prison camp in Texas as her criminal case generates renewed public attention.

The federal Bureau of Prisons said Friday that Maxwell had been transferred to Bryan but did not explain the circumstances. Her attorney confirmed the move but also declined to discuss the reasons for it.
[BECOME A PAID SUBSCRIBER: $7/mo](https://substack.com/redirect/6dfde87e-75d3-4816-90d4-9db10dbe94ba?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
Maxwell was convicted in 2021 of helping Epstein sexually abuse underage girls and was [sentenced to 20 years in prison](https://substack.com/redirect/762ef3ee-a505-43b7-bf16-142aeac747a7?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). She had been held at a prison in Tallahassee, Fla., until her transfer to the camp in Texas, where other inmates include [Theranos founder](https://substack.com/redirect/0e131f7f-8401-465b-9517-653ef04c5a62?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) **Elizabeth Holmes** and **Jen Shah** of _The Real Housewives of Salt Lake City_.

Minimum-security federal prison camps house inmates the Bureau of Prisons considers to be the lowest security risk. Some don't even have fences.
[BECOME A PAID SUBSCRIBER: $70/yr](https://substack.com/redirect/6dfde87e-75d3-4816-90d4-9db10dbe94ba?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
The prison camps were originally designed with low security to make operations easier and to allow inmates tasked with performing work at the prison, like landscaping and maintenance, to avoid repeatedly checking in and out of a main prison facility.

Maxwell's case has been the subject of heightened public focus since an outcry over the Justice Department's statement last month saying that it would not be releasing any additional documents from the Epstein sex trafficking investigation.

Since then, administration officials have suggested they are promoting transparency in the case, including by requesting the unsealing of grand jury transcripts.

Maxwell was interviewed at a Florida courthouse over two days last week by Deputy Attorney General **Todd Blanche**.

The [House Oversight Committee](https://substack.com/redirect/353c4ad5-9344-4c46-a89f-6e2eece477c9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) has separately said that it wants to speak with Maxwell as well. Her lawyers said this week that she would be open to an interview but only if the panel were to give her immunity from prosecution for anything she said." [AP](https://substack.com/redirect/472418a4-759b-4da6-9a0d-af563b90ea2e?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
* * *
* **#TXLEGE**

**" Takeaways from the heated July 4 flood hearing: Kerr County officials grilled over absences, delays," **Houston Chronicle's**Amanda Drane** ,**Neena Satija** and**Megan Kimble** \-- "Flood relief is one of many items the Texas Legislature is taking up in the 30-day special session called by Gov. **Greg Abbott**. Lawmakers have yet to put forward a comprehensive agenda, but Thursday's testimony could help shape legislation that is eventually filed.

Here are the key takeaways so far:

**Dan Patrick called out Kerr County Judge Rob Kelly**

Lt. Gov. **Dan Patrick** said he was in Kerr County on July 4 and had a series of meetings with county officials, yet never saw County Judge **Rob Kelly** that day.

"This is about as bad a day as you can have, and I've had a lot of bad days. We didn't just lose people; we didn't know where people were," Patrick said. "And you weren't here."

Kelly had earlier testified he was staying at his lake home in Travis County overnight as the flooding began, but headed back to Kerr County after he woke up on July 4. At one point that day, Kelly said, he went home to check on his wife.

Patrick told the judge he should have been at the county's emergency center.

"I don't know where you were on Day One, on July 4, but you should have been here. You should have been here directing that response. That is your responsibility ... Everyone was here that day working their ass off and you were nowhere to be found."

**Local officials were asleep, out sick when flooding started**

The three top emergency officials in Kerr County all testified they were not working until after the floods began.

County Judge Kelly testified he woke up to phone calls and messages from Texas Division of Emergency Management **Nim Kidd** , Kerr County Sheriff **Larry Leitha** and Kerrville City Manager **Dalton Rice** in the "5, 5:30 range" on the morning of July 4.

By then, he said, flooding [had already overtaken Camp Mystic](https://substack.com/redirect/eeaeb956-61f9-4cc5-95df-5c5929ba069c?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) and other camps.

He said he received no "no alerts suggesting an extreme weather event was imminent," even though he gets a lot of alerts about weather.

"Something I could never have imagined," he said, adding the National Weather Service didn't forecast this "in a timely manner."

Leitha said he woke up at 4:20 a.m.: "I started calling people out. A little later, I did talk to the judge (Kelly)."

**W.B. "Dub" Thomas**, the county's emergency management coordinator, said he was ill and on paid time off on July 3 in the run-up to the deadly storm. He said he was awoken at 5:30 a.m. on July 4 by a caller asking him to respond to the floods.

"Based on the data we had at the time, there was no indication that a catastrophic flood was imminent," Thomas said.

The three top emergency management officials for Kerr County -- the county judge, the sheriff, and emergency management coordinator -- pointed out numerous times that some emergency responders were awake and responding to the flooding as early as 3 a.m. But lawmakers from both parties pushed back on that defense.

"The three guys in Kerr County who were responsible for sounding the alarm were effectively unavailable. Am I hearing that right?" asked state Rep. **Ann Johnson** , D-Houston.

"Knowing now what you know, is there a protocol that needs to be put in place? That, if the three folks who are responsible at this moment are not available for whatever reason, what should we do?" Johnson asked.

"Yes ma'am, we can look at it real hard," Leitha responded. "Maybe they can call me earlier."

State Rep. **Drew Darby** , R-San Angelo, echoed Johnson's concerns and said he found the county officials' answers "very disturbing."

"We have people dying," he said, "and yeah, we have people charged with responsibility for their protection -- but they're in their beds, they're sick, they're waiting for somebody else."

Johnson also asked Leitha and Thomas if they were aware that as early as 2 a.m., according to reports she said she'd heard, "there were little girls with water around their feet" at Camp Mystic on the Guadalupe River.

They said no.

"I've had discussions with the camps before, and the process was, they need to notify the sheriff's office," Thomas said. Leitha added that he had no indication his office had received any such notifications at that time.

**Kerr County criticized over delayed alerts, lack of evacuation notice**

Under Texas law, Kelly, the county judge, would have been responsible for deciding whether or not to issue evacuation orders for the county. Asked why he did not, he told lawmakers that it was "too late" and would have resulted in "one heck of a traffic jam" just as waters were overtaking roadways.

State Sen. **Paul Bettencourt** , R-Houston, said he understood that evacuations by road could have been too dangerous, but believed something still could have been done as weather forecasters were issuing flood warnings, particularly after an emergency alert went out not long after 4 a.m.

"Would there not have been a recognition of the obvious that there needs to be an alert that says, effectively, get to higher ground?" Bettencourt said.

"I agree," Kelly responded. Left unaddressed in the exchange was the fact that Kelly had apparently been asleep at that point and so would not have been able to make such a call.

Officials also acknowledged that a "CodeRed" alert regarding the flooding, which people in Kerr County could have received on their phones as long as they hadn't opted out, wasn't sent until 5:01 a.m - more than 40 minutes after a dispatcher had requested that it be sent.

**Timeline of flood response emerges**

For the first time, local officials with the City of Kerrville and Kerr County publicly provided a more detailed account of when emergency dispatchers began receiving urgent calls in the early morning hours of July 4.

At 3:34 a.m. -- more than two hours after the National Weather Service had issued a flood alert for portions of Kerr County -- emergency dispatchers received the first phone call from a family that was "on a roof requesting air evacuation" that led county officials to realize they had an "all hands on deck" situation, Leitha said.

After that, call volume increased quickly, according to timelines provided by local officials. In the next hour, dispatchers began hearing from children trapped in flooding homes. Because of rising waters, some sheriff's deputies became stranded, too. Calls from Camp Mystic came in at 3:57 a.m., Leitha said, reporting people were "stranded on a hill seeking evacuation."

While some Kerr County sheriff's deputies and other area first responders were awake at that point and saved many lives, Leitha said, it appeared that they were doing so while he and the county's emergency management official -- emergency coordinator Thomas -- were not yet awake. Still, Leitha reiterated that even weather forecasters had not foreseen the level of danger.

As an example, he noted that at 4:32 a.m. -- a few minutes after a sheriff's deputy reported hearing "kids screaming in the river" -- the National Weather Service called the sheriff's office to ask if roads were closed and if flooding was occurring.

Leitha paused and shook his head after delivering that testimony to lawmakers.

**Flood itself 'worth studying'**

Kerr County commissioned an independent hydrology assessment that confirms that "this was a 1,000-year flood," Kerr County Judge Kelly said. He described the event as "very much worth studying." It also sat "in a location with very few rain monitors or flood gauges."

The storm was "not typical for our weather patterns" because it stayed, "it did not pass through, it overwhelmed the watershed," he said.

"This was not foreseeable," Kelly said. It "reveals the urgent need for stronger tools, better detection, and modernized infrastructure."

**Lawmakers again grill leaders of the Upper Guadalupe River Authority**

Last week, lawmakers blasted the general manager of the Upper Guadalupe River Authority -- which covers the Guadalupe River in Kerr County alone -- for [choosing to cut property taxes rather than modernize](https://substack.com/redirect/b4e566e5-0cb8-43cb-837f-1f5985009913?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) its flood warning system, citing an investigation by the Houston Chronicle.

On Thursday, Darby, a Republican from San Angelo, again grilled UGRA board chair **William Rector** over that decision. "It was recognized through studies that you paid for that there was a need for an early warning system, in your words, an alert system would have saved lives. And yet you didn't do anything about it," Darby said.

Rector, who was appointed by Abbott said that the river authority had grown so frustrated with its efforts to secure funding for flood warning that "we said we're going to do it ourselves. We're going to just take the bull by the horn. We're going to create our own system," he said.

Rector said that in January, UGRA issued a request for proposals to install such a system. "By June 4, we had actually hired a contractor, signed a contract to begin creating a system with our own money that would support flood warning."

But the UGRA has so far approved little more than a "software system" consisting of a public-facing dashboard, rather than additional river gauges to collect more data, Rector confirmed during the hearing. Earlier this year the board approved spending less than $73,000 on the project, [documents show](https://substack.com/redirect/3b8e3c6c-68c3-4845-b96b-69a565b650f6?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).

Rector told lawmakers Thursday that the river authority more recently committed to spending $1.5 million from its reserves to conduct further studies on the issue.

State Rep. **Ken King** , a Republican and the chairman of the House committee, noted that UGRA's board is appointed by the governor.

"So you're not accountable to the voters of Kerr County?" he asked. "They couldn't not elect you and take you off the board, if they didn't think you were doing a good job?"

"That's correct," Rector said.

State Sen. **Donna Campbell** , a Republican from New Braunfels, suggested that the Upper Guadalupe River Authority might be absorbed by the larger Guadalupe Blanco River Authority, which covers 10 counties downstream.

"Guadalupe Blanco has water production and sewer services they can sell so if we were merged you would be asking people downstream to support us in Kerr County," Rector said.

"It's a river authority that is managed for one county only and I'm not sure there weren't things that could have been done over the years ... that possibly could have helped," Campbell said.

**Residents plead for more help with recovery**

Residents told harrowing stories of escaping rapidly rising July 4 flood waters and asked lawmakers to spend more on recovery efforts -- still ongoing weeks later -- and invest in warning systems, evacuation routes and more to save lives in future storms.

**Ann Carr** of Ingram told the panel she lives on Ingram Lake, which is still so full of debris it has become a "toxic pit." She said she has spoken to divers who have been in the lake and told her there are still bodies in the water. And she said an oil slick "continues to bubble up right in front of our property" from a submerged vehicle.

"We have six feet of rubble that has invaded our lake, and it's become a graveyard," Carr said.

She urged the state to dip into its massive rainy day fund to drain the lake and clean it out, arguing it is urgent for the survival of the community.

"That lake in Ingram is the heart of our city," Carr said. "It brings money into our city. We have people that come in and boat, fish. They spend money at the restaurants. We're a poor community, and we need that lake drained."

**Nancy Zdunkewicz** told the panel her family awoke at 3 a.m. and the flood water was already at their house in Hunt, about 40 feet above the normal water level.

Her family tried to get to higher ground, but was instead forced to climb into trees on their property and wait out the flood, watching as cars and other trees were washed away.

She called for more evacuation routes and warning systems.

"This community really needs help with recovery, and we need preparation for the next flood," Zdunkewicz said. "Had there been early detection of the rising waters and sirens, we may have been able to leave in time to get to higher ground safely."

**County officials request more resources**

Kelly said his county would need a flood warning and detection system to better predict future floods, more interconnectivity to address cell phone dead zones, and more resources for emergency management to better address future floods.

Much of the western portion of the county has very poor cell service, he said.

"Even if people wanted to call .. and warn folks ... they didn't have the ability," he said.

Rice said there needs to be investments in localized predictive weather modeling and expanded broadband infrastructure.

"Kerrville will rebuild, but we need the tools and the funding to do it right," he said.

**Texas ' top GOP leaders are in attendance**

Lt. Gov. Patrick and House Speaker **Dustin Burrows** don't serve on legislative committees and usually don't attend public hearings, but they showed up to this one in Kerrville.

In opening statements, Patrick, a Republican who presides over the state Senate, said this hearing is just the beginning.

"We will pass bills dealing with the immediacy of things that we can address now, but we will be doing this in the next session and the session after that," he told the crowd. "We will not quit until you feel like we've done everything that we can do that you've asked us to do."

Burrows, R-Lubbock, encouraged officials to be forthright in their testimony.

"We cannot come up with solutions, we cannot try to improve things, if we don't know everything that you can share with us," he said." [Hou Chron](https://substack.com/redirect/88200be2-a14f-4492-80c3-0809d1b404d9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

TOP NEWS " Ghislaine Maxwell transferred to prison camp in Texas," via AP** -- "Jeffrey Epstein 's former girlfriend, Ghislaine Maxwell , has been moved from a federal prison in Florida to a prison camp in Texas as her criminal case generates renewed public attention. The federal Bureau of Prisons said Friday that Maxwell had been transferred to Bryan but did not explain the circumstances. Her attorney confirmed the move but also declined to discuss the reasons for it. Maxwell was convicted in 2021 of helping Epstein sexually abuse underage girls and was . She had been held at a prison in Tallahassee, Fla., until her transfer to the camp in Texas, where other inmates include Elizabeth Holmes and Jen Shah of The Real Housewives of Salt Lake City. Minimum-security federal prison camps house inmates the Bureau of Prisons considers to be the lowest security risk. Some don't even have fences. The prison camps were originally designed with low security to make operations easier and to allow inmates tasked with performing work at the prison, like landscaping and maintenance, to avoid repeatedly checking in and out of a main prison facility. Maxwell's case has been the subject of heightened public focus since an outcry over the Justice Department's statement last month saying that it would not be releasing any additional documents from the Epstein sex trafficking investigation. Since then, administration officials have suggested they are promoting transparency in the case, including by requesting the unsealing of grand jury transcripts. Maxwell was interviewed at a Florida courthouse over two days last week by Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche. The has separately said that it wants to speak with Maxwell as well. Her lawyers said this week that she would be open to an interview but only if the panel were to give her immunity from prosecution for anything she said." #TXLEGE " Takeaways from the heated July 4 flood hearing: Kerr County officials grilled over absences, delays," Houston Chronicle'sAmanda Drane ,Neena Satija andMegan Kimble \-- "Flood relief is one of many items the Texas Legislature is taking up in the 30-day special session called by Gov. Greg Abbott. Lawmakers have yet to put forward a comprehensive agenda, but Thursday's testimony could help shape legislation that is eventually filed. Here are the key takeaways so far: Dan Patrick called out Kerr County Judge Rob Kelly Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick said he was in Kerr County on July 4 and had a series of meetings with county officials, yet never saw County Judge Rob Kelly that day. "This is about as bad a day as you can have, and I've had a lot of bad days. We didn't just lose people; we didn't know where people were," Patrick said. "And you weren't here." Kelly had earlier testified he was staying at his lake home in Travis County overnight as the flooding began, but headed back to Kerr County after he woke up on July 4. At one point that day, Kelly said, he went home to check on his wife. Patrick told the judge he should have been at the county's emergency center. "I don't know where you were on Day One, on July 4, but you should have been here. You should have been here directing that response. That is your responsibility ... Everyone was here that day working their ass off and you were nowhere to be found." Local officials were asleep, out sick when flooding started The three top emergency officials in Kerr County all testified they were not working until after the floods began. County Judge Kelly testified he woke up to phone calls and messages from Texas Division of Emergency Management Nim Kidd , Kerr County Sheriff Larry Leitha and Kerrville City Manager Dalton Rice in the "5, 5:30 range" on the morning of July 4. By then, he said, flooding and other camps. He said he received no "no alerts suggesting an extreme weather event was imminent," even though he gets a lot of alerts about weather. "Something I could never have imagined," he said, adding the National Weather Service didn't forecast this "in a timely manner." Leitha said he woke up at 4:20 a.m.: "I started calling people out. A little later, I did talk to the judge (Kelly)." W.B. "Dub" Thomas, the county's emergency management coordinator, said he was ill and on paid time off on July 3 in the run-up to the deadly storm. He said he was awoken at 5:30 a.m. on July 4 by a caller asking him to respond to the floods. "Based on the data we had at the time, there was no indication that a catastrophic flood was imminent," Thomas said. The three top emergency management officials for Kerr County -- the county judge, the sheriff, and emergency management coordinator -- pointed out numerous times that some emergency responders were awake and responding to the flooding as early as 3 a.m. But lawmakers from both parties pushed back on that defense. "The three guys in Kerr County who were responsible for sounding the alarm were effectively unavailable. Am I hearing that right?" asked state Rep. Ann Johnson , D-Houston. "Knowing now what you know, is there a protocol that needs to be put in place? That, if the three folks who are responsible at this moment are not available for whatever reason, what should we do?" Johnson asked. "Yes ma'am, we can look at it real hard," Leitha responded. "Maybe they can call me earlier." State Rep. Drew Darby , R-San Angelo, echoed Johnson's concerns and said he found the county officials' answers "very disturbing." "We have people dying," he said, "and yeah, we have people charged with responsibility for their protection -- but they're in their beds, they're sick, they're waiting for somebody else." Johnson also asked Leitha and Thomas if they were aware that as early as 2 a.m., according to reports she said she'd heard, "there were little girls with water around their feet" at Camp Mystic on the Guadalupe River. They said no. "I've had discussions with the camps before, and the process was, they need to notify the sheriff's office," Thomas said. Leitha added that he had no indication his office had received any such notifications at that time. Kerr County criticized over delayed alerts, lack of evacuation notice Under Texas law, Kelly, the county judge, would have been responsible for deciding whether or not to issue evacuation orders for the county. Asked why he did not, he told lawmakers that it was "too late" and would have resulted in "one heck of a traffic jam" just as waters were overtaking roadways. State Sen. Paul Bettencourt , R-Houston, said he understood that evacuations by road could have been too dangerous, but believed something still could have been done as weather forecasters were issuing flood warnings, particularly after an emergency alert went out not long after 4 a.m. "Would there not have been a recognition of the obvious that there needs to be an alert that says, effectively, get to higher ground?" Bettencourt said. "I agree," Kelly responded. Left unaddressed in the exchange was the fact that Kelly had apparently been asleep at that point and so would not have been able to make such a call. Officials also acknowledged that a "CodeRed" alert regarding the flooding, which people in Kerr County could have received on their phones as long as they hadn't opted out, wasn't sent until 5:01 a.m - more than 40 minutes after a dispatcher had requested that it be sent. Timeline of flood response emerges For the first time, local officials with the City of Kerrville and Kerr County publicly provided a more detailed account of when emergency dispatchers began receiving urgent calls in the early morning hours of July 4. At 3:34 a.m. -- more than two hours after the National Weather Service had issued a flood alert for portions of Kerr County -- emergency dispatchers received the first phone call from a family that was "on a roof requesting air evacuation" that led county officials to realize they had an "all hands on deck" situation, Leitha said. After that, call volume increased quickly, according to timelines provided by local officials. In the next hour, dispatchers began hearing from children trapped in flooding homes. Because of rising waters, some sheriff's deputies became stranded, too. Calls from Camp Mystic came in at 3:57 a.m., Leitha said, reporting people were "stranded on a hill seeking evacuation." While some Kerr County sheriff's deputies and other area first responders were awake at that point and saved many lives, Leitha said, it appeared that they were doing so while he and the county's emergency management official -- emergency coordinator Thomas -- were not yet awake. Still, Leitha reiterated that even weather forecasters had not foreseen the level of danger. As an example, he noted that at 4:32 a.m. -- a few minutes after a sheriff's deputy reported hearing "kids screaming in the river" -- the National Weather Service called the sheriff's office to ask if roads were closed and if flooding was occurring. Leitha paused and shook his head after delivering that testimony to lawmakers. Flood itself 'worth studying' Kerr County commissioned an independent hydrology assessment that confirms that "this was a 1,000-year flood," Kerr County Judge Kelly said. He described the event as "very much worth studying." It also sat "in a location with very few rain monitors or flood gauges." The storm was "not typical for our weather patterns" because it stayed, "it did not pass through, it overwhelmed the watershed," he said. "This was not foreseeable," Kelly said. It "reveals the urgent need for stronger tools, better detection, and modernized infrastructure." Lawmakers again grill leaders of the Upper Guadalupe River Authority Last week, lawmakers blasted the general manager of the Upper Guadalupe River Authority -- which covers the Guadalupe River in Kerr County alone -- for its flood warning system, citing an investigation by the Houston Chronicle. On Thursday, Darby, a Republican from San Angelo, again grilled UGRA board chair William Rector over that decision. "It was recognized through studies that you paid for that there was a need for an early warning system, in your words, an alert system would have saved lives. And yet you didn't do anything about it," Darby said. Rector, who was appointed by Abbott said that the river authority had grown so frustrated with its efforts to secure funding for flood warning that "we said we're going to do it ourselves. We're going to just take the bull by the horn. We're going to create our own system," he said. Rector said that in January, UGRA issued a request for proposals to install such a system. "By June 4, we had actually hired a contractor, signed a contract to begin creating a system with our own money that would support flood warning." But the UGRA has so far approved little more than a "software system" consisting of a public-facing dashboard, rather than additional river gauges to collect more data, Rector confirmed during the hearing. Earlier this year the board approved spending less than $73,000 on the project, . Rector told lawmakers Thursday that the river authority more recently committed to spending $1.5 million from its reserves to conduct further studies on the issue. State Rep. Ken King , a Republican and the chairman of the House committee, noted that UGRA's board is appointed by the governor. "So you're not accountable to the voters of Kerr County?" he asked. "They couldn't not elect you and take you off the board, if they didn't think you were doing a good job?" "That's correct," Rector said. State Sen. Donna Campbell , a Republican from New Braunfels, suggested that the Upper Guadalupe River Authority might be absorbed by the larger Guadalupe Blanco River Authority, which covers 10 counties downstream. "Guadalupe Blanco has water production and sewer services they can sell so if we were merged you would be asking people downstream to support us in Kerr County," Rector said. "It's a river authority that is managed for one county only and I'm not sure there weren't things that could have been done over the years ... that possibly could have helped," Campbell said. Residents plead for more help with recovery Residents told harrowing stories of escaping rapidly rising July 4 flood waters and asked lawmakers to spend more on recovery efforts -- still ongoing weeks later -- and invest in warning systems, evacuation routes and more to save lives in future storms. Ann Carr of Ingram told the panel she lives on Ingram Lake, which is still so full of debris it has become a "toxic pit." She said she has spoken to divers who have been in the lake and told her there are still bodies in the water. And she said an oil slick "continues to bubble up right in front of our property" from a submerged vehicle. "We have six feet of rubble that has invaded our lake, and it's become a graveyard," Carr said. She urged the state to dip into its massive rainy day fund to drain the lake and clean it out, arguing it is urgent for the survival of the community. "That lake in Ingram is the heart of our city," Carr said. "It brings money into our city. We have people that come in and boat, fish. They spend money at the restaurants. We're a poor community, and we need that lake drained." Nancy Zdunkewicz told the panel her family awoke at 3 a.m. and the flood water was already at their house in Hunt, about 40 feet above the normal water level. Her family tried to get to higher ground, but was instead forced to climb into trees on their property and wait out the flood, watching as cars and other trees were washed away. She called for more evacuation routes and warning systems. "This community really needs help with recovery, and we need preparation for the next flood," Zdunkewicz said. "Had there been early detection of the rising waters and sirens, we may have been able to leave in time to get to higher ground safely." County officials request more resources Kelly said his county would need a flood warning and detection system to better predict future floods, more interconnectivity to address cell phone dead zones, and more resources for emergency management to better address future floods. Much of the western portion of the county has very poor cell service, he said. "Even if people wanted to call .. and warn folks ... they didn't have the ability," he said. Rice said there needs to be investments in localized predictive weather modeling and expanded broadband infrastructure. "Kerrville will rebuild, but we need the tools and the funding to do it right," he said. Texas ' top GOP leaders are in attendance Lt. Gov. Patrick and House Speaker Dustin Burrows don't serve on legislative committees and usually don't attend public hearings, but they showed up to this one in Kerrville. In opening statements, Patrick, a Republican who presides over the state Senate, said this hearing is just the beginning. "We will pass bills dealing with the immediacy of things that we can address now, but we will be doing this in the next session and the session after that," he told the crowd. "We will not quit until you feel like we've done everything that we can do that you've asked us to do." Burrows, R-Lubbock, encouraged officials to be forthright in their testimony. "We cannot come up with solutions, we cannot try to improve things, if we don't know everything that you can share with us," he said." * **#TXLEGE**

**“Takeaways from the heated July 4 flood hearing: Kerr County officials grilled over absences, delays,”** Houston Chronicle's**Amanda Drane** ,**Neena Satija** and**Megan Kimble** \-- "Flood relief is one of many items the Texas Legislature is taking up in the 30-day special session called by Gov. **Greg Abbott**. Lawmakers have yet to put forward a comprehensive agenda, but Thursday’s testimony could help shape legislation that is eventually filed.

Here are the key takeaways so far:

**Dan Patrick called out Kerr County Judge Rob Kelly**

Lt. Gov. **Dan Patrick** said he was in Kerr County on July 4 and had a series of meetings with county officials, yet never saw County Judge **Rob Kelly** that day.

“This is about as bad a day as you can have, and I’ve had a lot of bad days. We didn't just lose people; we didn’t know where people were,” Patrick said. “And you weren't here.”

Kelly had earlier testified he was staying at his lake home in Travis County overnight as the flooding began, but headed back to Kerr County after he woke up on July 4. At one point that day, Kelly said, he went home to check on his wife.

Patrick told the judge he should have been at the county's emergency center.

“I don’t know where you were on Day One, on July 4, but you should have been here. You should have been here directing that response. That is your responsibility ... Everyone was here that day working their ass off and you were nowhere to be found.”

**Local officials were asleep, out sick when flooding started**

The three top emergency officials in Kerr County all testified they were not working until after the floods began.

County Judge Kelly testified he woke up to phone calls and messages from Texas Division of Emergency Management **Nim Kidd** , Kerr County Sheriff **Larry Leitha** and Kerrville City Manager **Dalton Rice** in the “5, 5:30 range” on the morning of July 4.

By then, he said, flooding [had already overtaken Camp Mystic](https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.xjnMFfva-73dHOne5Rc0eNCDHcRdv524vOoQmRT6HI5xza0xQ6OamIlucfRqu24ZTy12FX7th_AXTOspBH_xV4WPF9t4zELL6yRRVQCgGIblro-mgw76QwZyMC1gWeLwOVaQRy1ulypqDjefZ7u-rtRnd26_3lkmt3YpX9KNFpRJL0K0sLBG-RDBKexbuisA_ID8gBv1PrBgMy5RKEpC_8u6AbcJ2xww5a4OVZDx_1Kmpq4aGPa0io1EmHMtys6GlFLPnUcWmFTCX2zKweuHbd8OR_hsnYZuUEBe6iDldF3R9hBhmPhhDTP7PcRFeL7SPnJcCc84ipOj2AvpKzmkPauaqbN48YLG_dh1h-w429mBA5RmXmWHt37ayWG1UNPUsYfJ_rLRgvA31TjEiCBe_n5pG0kxy7B9AGH0hx2SoQxE85Uffke7ruB7Kh03eIoITKtRzjXStgWOTbc3448MD_zOkWdV6tt7CUWuUW4Q2QDTLR3xeicr_YHrxLLL55CjifXY3zJ5yRq5FAq1J20qykHcSNGyC77gZSLW49PDA0E/4io/74uxZnmJQhaE_tV8USR8Dg/h15/h001.tqWrqiavVej3lIZXG8PFLA3vaXCijzBZTT0dlFmH5vM) and other camps.

He said he received no “no alerts suggesting an extreme weather event was imminent,” even though he gets a lot of alerts about weather.

“Something I could never have imagined,” he said, adding the National Weather Service didn’t forecast this “in a timely manner.”

Leitha said he woke up at 4:20 a.m.: "I started calling people out. A little later, I did talk to the judge (Kelly)."

**W.B. “Dub” Thomas** , the county’s emergency management coordinator, said he was ill and on paid time off on July 3 in the run-up to the deadly storm. He said he was awoken at 5:30 a.m. on July 4 by a caller asking him to respond to the floods.

“Based on the data we had at the time, there was no indication that a catastrophic flood was imminent,” Thomas said.

The three top emergency management officials for Kerr County — the county judge, the sheriff, and emergency management coordinator — pointed out numerous times that some emergency responders were awake and responding to the flooding as early as 3 a.m. But lawmakers from both parties pushed back on that defense.

“The three guys in Kerr County who were responsible for sounding the alarm were effectively unavailable. Am I hearing that right?” asked state Rep. **Ann Johnson** , D-Houston.

“Knowing now what you know, is there a protocol that needs to be put in place? That, if the three folks who are responsible at this moment are not available for whatever reason, what should we do?” Johnson asked.

“Yes ma’am, we can look at it real hard,” Leitha responded. “Maybe they can call me earlier.”

State Rep. **Drew Darby** , R-San Angelo, echoed Johnson’s concerns and said he found the county officials’ answers “very disturbing.”

“We have people dying,” he said, “and yeah, we have people charged with responsibility for their protection — but they’re in their beds, they’re sick, they’re waiting for somebody else.”

Johnson also asked Leitha and Thomas if they were aware that as early as 2 a.m., according to reports she said she’d heard, “there were little girls with water around their feet” at Camp Mystic on the Guadalupe River.

They said no.

“I’ve had discussions with the camps before, and the process was, they need to notify the sheriff’s office,” Thomas said. Leitha added that he had no indication his office had received any such notifications at that time.

**Kerr County criticized over delayed alerts, lack of evacuation notice**

Under Texas law, Kelly, the county judge, would have been responsible for deciding whether or not to issue evacuation orders for the county. Asked why he did not, he told lawmakers that it was “too late” and would have resulted in “one heck of a traffic jam” just as waters were overtaking roadways.

State Sen. **Paul Bettencourt** , R-Houston, said he understood that evacuations by road could have been too dangerous, but believed something still could have been done as weather forecasters were issuing flood warnings, particularly after an emergency alert went out not long after 4 a.m.

“Would there not have been a recognition of the obvious that there needs to be an alert that says, effectively, get to higher ground?” Bettencourt said.

“I agree,” Kelly responded. Left unaddressed in the exchange was the fact that Kelly had apparently been asleep at that point and so would not have been able to make such a call.

Officials also acknowledged that a “CodeRed” alert regarding the flooding, which people in Kerr County could have received on their phones as long as they hadn’t opted out, wasn’t sent until 5:01 a.m – more than 40 minutes after a dispatcher had requested that it be sent.

**Timeline of flood response emerges**

For the first time, local officials with the City of Kerrville and Kerr County publicly provided a more detailed account of when emergency dispatchers began receiving urgent calls in the early morning hours of July 4.

At 3:34 a.m. — more than two hours after the National Weather Service had issued a flood alert for portions of Kerr County — emergency dispatchers received the first phone call from a family that was “on a roof requesting air evacuation” that led county officials to realize they had an “all hands on deck” situation, Leitha said.

After that, call volume increased quickly, according to timelines provided by local officials. In the next hour, dispatchers began hearing from children trapped in flooding homes. Because of rising waters, some sheriff’s deputies became stranded, too. Calls from Camp Mystic came in at 3:57 a.m., Leitha said, reporting people were “stranded on a hill seeking evacuation.”

While some Kerr County sheriff’s deputies and other area first responders were awake at that point and saved many lives, Leitha said, it appeared that they were doing so while he and the county’s emergency management official — emergency coordinator Thomas — were not yet awake. Still, Leitha reiterated that even weather forecasters had not foreseen the level of danger.

As an example, he noted that at 4:32 a.m. — a few minutes after a sheriff’s deputy reported hearing “kids screaming in the river” — the National Weather Service called the sheriff’s office to ask if roads were closed and if flooding was occurring.

Leitha paused and shook his head after delivering that testimony to lawmakers.

**Flood itself ‘worth studying’**

Kerr County commissioned an independent hydrology assessment that confirms that “this was a 1,000-year flood,” Kerr County Judge Kelly said. He described the event as “very much worth studying.” It also sat “in a location with very few rain monitors or flood gauges.”

The storm was “not typical for our weather patterns” because it stayed, “it did not pass through, it overwhelmed the watershed,” he said.

“This was not foreseeable,” Kelly said. It “reveals the urgent need for stronger tools, better detection, and modernized infrastructure.”

**Lawmakers again grill leaders of the Upper Guadalupe River Authority**

Last week, lawmakers blasted the general manager of the Upper Guadalupe River Authority — which covers the Guadalupe River in Kerr County alone — for [choosing to cut property taxes rather than modernize](https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.xjnMFfva-73dHOne5Rc0eNCDHcRdv524vOoQmRT6HI5xza0xQ6OamIlucfRqu24ZTy12FX7th_AXTOspBH_xVyfu6Di-TJdYKPgUoqYp_a6WxF8QqSHEg65BO9aoqnryNV7ZJniDc_BuQBDSYoGAeTslKZjp7mvcrPujX9_SNsYJJMwi6M961VO8j8gQ_FZmdCvhScEEETkmCFdVraIBMW093ZWd4Ft-KYzpVqochWf8S5Cgr9e3W1BblfUuBu68rztGOCDa7yH5WQkSmgeB-K3F8WU-vK2PHqdhLXLg4fsWvikRWtFfoF2iz6O8nIkQjuqygstxhcHXYGR-FsMDc1CT4FtwWA_JHg9Phj7zDzKQpEEkb4kfB5zUu_ENhArB8xD4gt-Z_YnFeNQ0BVMpDrKhtFA7h0_QFeOf5Bgd7xAPP_wtUa8JqEpwjPdlawM-UTZzOxxaXPzOnrq-3DPjuft-CyGNYtLub15yiDxyG3dtBiMugLqTVGMrUwtD3TuZLD7J4-9eU8dWG4Eyz4sjARKSfarmv6kc7_TieEiNSDQ/4io/74uxZnmJQhaE_tV8USR8Dg/h16/h001.ftK9KmKuIYfQ_BHhX6JG9FF_Beun5BnSlEvKu94gcug) its flood warning system, citing an investigation by the Houston Chronicle.

On Thursday, Darby, a Republican from San Angelo, again grilled UGRA board chair **William Rector** over that decision. “It was recognized through studies that you paid for that there was a need for an early warning system, in your words, an alert system would have saved lives. And yet you didn't do anything about it,” Darby said.

Rector, who was appointed by Abbott said that the river authority had grown so frustrated with its efforts to secure funding for flood warning that “we said we're going to do it ourselves. We're going to just take the bull by the horn. We're going to create our own system,” he said.

Rector said that in January, UGRA issued a request for proposals to install such a system. “By June 4, we had actually hired a contractor, signed a contract to begin creating a system with our own money that would support flood warning.”

But the UGRA has so far approved little more than a “software system” consisting of a public-facing dashboard, rather than additional river gauges to collect more data, Rector confirmed during the hearing. Earlier this year the board approved spending less than $73,000 on the project, [documents show](https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.T-jDXkUxZtvkjyekbjTAlbJHpSoc-kGaZ88f4w-9ZfGEAoXg_rwkIIz46y69d4kMz9rpQFvLrdFTLn4UcBHAbNKA-HyteB3iLEfP9gfTlG1VF005T4XxFvBuCG1_NjuXf23sWEaKw9aoz0WoBaFgtpDtvhP9w8lgbLgWj4QBmqKJoHWsH-fcAcVazKRos1PmE4xSXhABBEk5WILsJHvfM0Hhj9ffIo2yPcBIRvAuylwFLxJemF_FkaEh8v4GiUN-_lXrhgCzGlH_BLQ8dZOaH3fp2utyZMZBOHHGj1ZAZiJ_oL1NhlSgEg3KrARu26b_ro6uNRTaz78SEl21y_tgcifs8FCscVk4KwxPoJQw555bgpLzaZujTb2FyM6MANRPOPe1v-331p0d7Gikc9TrLUiFgqPtLFGfNn3DmC5MGJiM22AoRaNmtNx5eOZYFBgFKPLfZ15s1tycnfkKaLcTM1pRuZhERf5AHg7cCGBS-xI/4io/74uxZnmJQhaE_tV8USR8Dg/h17/h001.tmmspyqJARnfDkQU1BYW1xI_ZBErA178JQ4cVzDwEMw).

Rector told lawmakers Thursday that the river authority more recently committed to spending $1.5 million from its reserves to conduct further studies on the issue.

State Rep. **Ken King** , a Republican and the chairman of the House committee, noted that UGRA’s board is appointed by the governor.

“So you're not accountable to the voters of Kerr County?” he asked. “They couldn't not elect you and take you off the board, if they didn't think you were doing a good job?”

“That’s correct,” Rector said.

State Sen. **Donna Campbell** , a Republican from New Braunfels, suggested that the Upper Guadalupe River Authority might be absorbed by the larger Guadalupe Blanco River Authority, which covers 10 counties downstream.

“Guadalupe Blanco has water production and sewer services they can sell so if we were merged you would be asking people downstream to support us in Kerr County,” Rector said.

“It’s a river authority that is managed for one county only and I’m not sure there weren’t things that could have been done over the years ... that possibly could have helped,” Campbell said.

**Residents plead for more help with recovery**

Residents told harrowing stories of escaping rapidly rising July 4 flood waters and asked lawmakers to spend more on recovery efforts — still ongoing weeks later — and invest in warning systems, evacuation routes and more to save lives in future storms.

**Ann Carr** of Ingram told the panel she lives on Ingram Lake, which is still so full of debris it has become a “toxic pit.” She said she has spoken to divers who have been in the lake and told her there are still bodies in the water. And she said an oil slick “continues to bubble up right in front of our property” from a submerged vehicle.

“We have six feet of rubble that has invaded our lake, and it's become a graveyard,” Carr said.

She urged the state to dip into its massive rainy day fund to drain the lake and clean it out, arguing it is urgent for the survival of the community.

“That lake in Ingram is the heart of our city,” Carr said. “It brings money into our city. We have people that come in and boat, fish. They spend money at the restaurants. We're a poor community, and we need that lake drained.”

**Nancy Zdunkewicz** told the panel her family awoke at 3 a.m. and the flood water was already at their house in Hunt, about 40 feet above the normal water level.

Her family tried to get to higher ground, but was instead forced to climb into trees on their property and wait out the flood, watching as cars and other trees were washed away.

She called for more evacuation routes and warning systems.

“This community really needs help with recovery, and we need preparation for the next flood,” Zdunkewicz said. “Had there been early detection of the rising waters and sirens, we may have been able to leave in time to get to higher ground safely.”

**County officials request more resources**

Kelly said his county would need a flood warning and detection system to better predict future floods, more interconnectivity to address cell phone dead zones, and more resources for emergency management to better address future floods.

Much of the western portion of the county has very poor cell service, he said.

“Even if people wanted to call .. and warn folks ... they didn’t have the ability,” he said.

Rice said there needs to be investments in localized predictive weather modeling and expanded broadband infrastructure.

”Kerrville will rebuild, but we need the tools and the funding to do it right,” he said.

**Texas’ top GOP leaders are in attendance**

Lt. Gov. Patrick and House Speaker **Dustin Burrows** don’t serve on legislative committees and usually don’t attend public hearings, but they showed up to this one in Kerrville.

In opening statements, Patrick, a Republican who presides over the state Senate, said this hearing is just the beginning.

“We will pass bills dealing with the immediacy of things that we can address now, but we will be doing this in the next session and the session after that,” he told the crowd. “We will not quit until you feel like we’ve done everything that we can do that you’ve asked us to do.”

Burrows, R-Lubbock, encouraged officials to be forthright in their testimony.

“We cannot come up with solutions, we cannot try to improve things, if we don’t know everything that you can share with us,” he said." Become a PAID subscriber for $5/mo or $50/yr | TOP NEWS “Ghislaine Maxwell transferred to prison camp in Texas,” via AP** — “Jeffrey Epstein’s former girlfriend, Ghislaine Maxwell , has been moved from a federal prison in Florida to a prison camp in Texas as her criminal case generates renewed public attention. The federal Bureau of Prisons said Friday that Maxwell had been transferred to Bryan but did not explain the circumstances. Her attorney confirmed the move but also declined to discuss the reasons for it. » Maxwell was convicted in 2021 of helping Epstein sexually abuse underage girls and was . She had been held at a prison in Tallahassee, Fla., until her transfer to the camp in Texas, where other inmates include Elizabeth Holmes and Jen Shah of The Real Housewives of Salt Lake City. Minimum-security federal prison camps house inmates the Bureau of Prisons considers to be the lowest security risk. Some don’t even have fences. » The prison camps were originally designed with low security to make operations easier and to allow inmates tasked with performing work at the prison, like landscaping and maintenance, to avoid repeatedly checking in and out of a main prison facility. Maxwell’s case has been the subject of heightened public focus since an outcry over the Justice Department’s statement last month saying that it would not be releasing any additional documents from the Epstein sex trafficking investigation. Since then, administration officials have suggested they are promoting transparency in the case, including by requesting the unsealing of grand jury transcripts. Maxwell was interviewed at a Florida courthouse over two days last week by Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche. The has separately said that it wants to speak with Maxwell as well. Her lawyers said this week that she would be open to an interview but only if the panel were to give her immunity from prosecution for anything she said." | #TXLEGE “Takeaways from the heated July 4 flood hearing: Kerr County officials grilled over absences, delays,” Houston Chronicle'sAmanda Drane ,Neena Satija andMegan Kimble \-- "Flood relief is one of many items the Texas Legislature is taking up in the 30-day special session called by Gov. Greg Abbott. Lawmakers have yet to put forward a comprehensive agenda, but Thursday’s testimony could help shape legislation that is eventually filed. Here are the key takeaways so far: Dan Patrick called out Kerr County Judge Rob Kelly Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick said he was in Kerr County on July 4 and had a series of meetings with county officials, yet never saw County Judge Rob Kelly that day. “This is about as bad a day as you can have, and I’ve had a lot of bad days. We didn't just lose people; we didn’t know where people were,” Patrick said. “And you weren't here.” Kelly had earlier testified he was staying at his lake home in Travis County overnight as the flooding began, but headed back to Kerr County after he woke up on July 4. At one point that day, Kelly said, he went home to check on his wife. Patrick told the judge he should have been at the county's emergency center. “I don’t know where you were on Day One, on July 4, but you should have been here. You should have been here directing that response. That is your responsibility ... Everyone was here that day working their ass off and you were nowhere to be found.” Local officials were asleep, out sick when flooding started The three top emergency officials in Kerr County all testified they were not working until after the floods began. County Judge Kelly testified he woke up to phone calls and messages from Texas Division of Emergency Management Nim Kidd , Kerr County Sheriff Larry Leitha and Kerrville City Manager Dalton Rice in the “5, 5:30 range” on the morning of July 4. By then, he said, flooding and other camps. He said he received no “no alerts suggesting an extreme weather event was imminent,” even though he gets a lot of alerts about weather. “Something I could never have imagined,” he said, adding the National Weather Service didn’t forecast this “in a timely manner.” Leitha said he woke up at 4:20 a.m.: "I started calling people out. A little later, I did talk to the judge (Kelly)." W.B. “Dub” Thomas , the county’s emergency management coordinator, said he was ill and on paid time off on July 3 in the run-up to the deadly storm. He said he was awoken at 5:30 a.m. on July 4 by a caller asking him to respond to the floods. “Based on the data we had at the time, there was no indication that a catastrophic flood was imminent,” Thomas said. The three top emergency management officials for Kerr County — the county judge, the sheriff, and emergency management coordinator — pointed out numerous times that some emergency responders were awake and responding to the flooding as early as 3 a.m. But lawmakers from both parties pushed back on that defense. “The three guys in Kerr County who were responsible for sounding the alarm were effectively unavailable. Am I hearing that right?” asked state Rep. Ann Johnson , D-Houston. “Knowing now what you know, is there a protocol that needs to be put in place? That, if the three folks who are responsible at this moment are not available for whatever reason, what should we do?” Johnson asked. “Yes ma’am, we can look at it real hard,” Leitha responded. “Maybe they can call me earlier.” State Rep. Drew Darby , R-San Angelo, echoed Johnson’s concerns and said he found the county officials’ answers “very disturbing.” “We have people dying,” he said, “and yeah, we have people charged with responsibility for their protection — but they’re in their beds, they’re sick, they’re waiting for somebody else.” Johnson also asked Leitha and Thomas if they were aware that as early as 2 a.m., according to reports she said she’d heard, “there were little girls with water around their feet” at Camp Mystic on the Guadalupe River. They said no. “I’ve had discussions with the camps before, and the process was, they need to notify the sheriff’s office,” Thomas said. Leitha added that he had no indication his office had received any such notifications at that time. Kerr County criticized over delayed alerts, lack of evacuation notice Under Texas law, Kelly, the county judge, would have been responsible for deciding whether or not to issue evacuation orders for the county. Asked why he did not, he told lawmakers that it was “too late” and would have resulted in “one heck of a traffic jam” just as waters were overtaking roadways. State Sen. Paul Bettencourt , R-Houston, said he understood that evacuations by road could have been too dangerous, but believed something still could have been done as weather forecasters were issuing flood warnings, particularly after an emergency alert went out not long after 4 a.m. “Would there not have been a recognition of the obvious that there needs to be an alert that says, effectively, get to higher ground?” Bettencourt said. “I agree,” Kelly responded. Left unaddressed in the exchange was the fact that Kelly had apparently been asleep at that point and so would not have been able to make such a call. Officials also acknowledged that a “CodeRed” alert regarding the flooding, which people in Kerr County could have received on their phones as long as they hadn’t opted out, wasn’t sent until 5:01 a.m – more than 40 minutes after a dispatcher had requested that it be sent. Timeline of flood response emerges For the first time, local officials with the City of Kerrville and Kerr County publicly provided a more detailed account of when emergency dispatchers began receiving urgent calls in the early morning hours of July 4. At 3:34 a.m. — more than two hours after the National Weather Service had issued a flood alert for portions of Kerr County — emergency dispatchers received the first phone call from a family that was “on a roof requesting air evacuation” that led county officials to realize they had an “all hands on deck” situation, Leitha said. After that, call volume increased quickly, according to timelines provided by local officials. In the next hour, dispatchers began hearing from children trapped in flooding homes. Because of rising waters, some sheriff’s deputies became stranded, too. Calls from Camp Mystic came in at 3:57 a.m., Leitha said, reporting people were “stranded on a hill seeking evacuation.” While some Kerr County sheriff’s deputies and other area first responders were awake at that point and saved many lives, Leitha said, it appeared that they were doing so while he and the county’s emergency management official — emergency coordinator Thomas — were not yet awake. Still, Leitha reiterated that even weather forecasters had not foreseen the level of danger. As an example, he noted that at 4:32 a.m. — a few minutes after a sheriff’s deputy reported hearing “kids screaming in the river” — the National Weather Service called the sheriff’s office to ask if roads were closed and if flooding was occurring. Leitha paused and shook his head after delivering that testimony to lawmakers. Flood itself ‘worth studying’ Kerr County commissioned an independent hydrology assessment that confirms that “this was a 1,000-year flood,” Kerr County Judge Kelly said. He described the event as “very much worth studying.” It also sat “in a location with very few rain monitors or flood gauges.” The storm was “not typical for our weather patterns” because it stayed, “it did not pass through, it overwhelmed the watershed,” he said. “This was not foreseeable,” Kelly said. It “reveals the urgent need for stronger tools, better detection, and modernized infrastructure.” Lawmakers again grill leaders of the Upper Guadalupe River Authority Last week, lawmakers blasted the general manager of the Upper Guadalupe River Authority — which covers the Guadalupe River in Kerr County alone — for its flood warning system, citing an investigation by the Houston Chronicle. On Thursday, Darby, a Republican from San Angelo, again grilled UGRA board chair William Rector over that decision. “It was recognized through studies that you paid for that there was a need for an early warning system, in your words, an alert system would have saved lives. And yet you didn't do anything about it,” Darby said. Rector, who was appointed by Abbott said that the river authority had grown so frustrated with its efforts to secure funding for flood warning that “we said we're going to do it ourselves. We're going to just take the bull by the horn. We're going to create our own system,” he said. Rector said that in January, UGRA issued a request for proposals to install such a system. “By June 4, we had actually hired a contractor, signed a contract to begin creating a system with our own money that would support flood warning.” But the UGRA has so far approved little more than a “software system” consisting of a public-facing dashboard, rather than additional river gauges to collect more data, Rector confirmed during the hearing. Earlier this year the board approved spending less than $73,000 on the project, . Rector told lawmakers Thursday that the river authority more recently committed to spending $1.5 million from its reserves to conduct further studies on the issue. State Rep. Ken King , a Republican and the chairman of the House committee, noted that UGRA’s board is appointed by the governor. “So you're not accountable to the voters of Kerr County?” he asked. “They couldn't not elect you and take you off the board, if they didn't think you were doing a good job?” “That’s correct,” Rector said. State Sen. Donna Campbell , a Republican from New Braunfels, suggested that the Upper Guadalupe River Authority might be absorbed by the larger Guadalupe Blanco River Authority, which covers 10 counties downstream. “Guadalupe Blanco has water production and sewer services they can sell so if we were merged you would be asking people downstream to support us in Kerr County,” Rector said. “It’s a river authority that is managed for one county only and I’m not sure there weren’t things that could have been done over the years ... that possibly could have helped,” Campbell said. Residents plead for more help with recovery Residents told harrowing stories of escaping rapidly rising July 4 flood waters and asked lawmakers to spend more on recovery efforts — still ongoing weeks later — and invest in warning systems, evacuation routes and more to save lives in future storms. Ann Carr of Ingram told the panel she lives on Ingram Lake, which is still so full of debris it has become a “toxic pit.” She said she has spoken to divers who have been in the lake and told her there are still bodies in the water. And she said an oil slick “continues to bubble up right in front of our property” from a submerged vehicle. “We have six feet of rubble that has invaded our lake, and it's become a graveyard,” Carr said. She urged the state to dip into its massive rainy day fund to drain the lake and clean it out, arguing it is urgent for the survival of the community. “That lake in Ingram is the heart of our city,” Carr said. “It brings money into our city. We have people that come in and boat, fish. They spend money at the restaurants. We're a poor community, and we need that lake drained.” Nancy Zdunkewicz told the panel her family awoke at 3 a.m. and the flood water was already at their house in Hunt, about 40 feet above the normal water level. Her family tried to get to higher ground, but was instead forced to climb into trees on their property and wait out the flood, watching as cars and other trees were washed away. She called for more evacuation routes and warning systems. “This community really needs help with recovery, and we need preparation for the next flood,” Zdunkewicz said. “Had there been early detection of the rising waters and sirens, we may have been able to leave in time to get to higher ground safely.” County officials request more resources Kelly said his county would need a flood warning and detection system to better predict future floods, more interconnectivity to address cell phone dead zones, and more resources for emergency management to better address future floods. Much of the western portion of the county has very poor cell service, he said. “Even if people wanted to call .. and warn folks ... they didn’t have the ability,” he said. Rice said there needs to be investments in localized predictive weather modeling and expanded broadband infrastructure. ”Kerrville will rebuild, but we need the tools and the funding to do it right,” he said. Texas’ top GOP leaders are in attendance Lt. Gov. Patrick and House Speaker Dustin Burrows don’t serve on legislative committees and usually don’t attend public hearings, but they showed up to this one in Kerrville. In opening statements, Patrick, a Republican who presides over the state Senate, said this hearing is just the beginning. “We will pass bills dealing with the immediacy of things that we can address now, but we will be doing this in the next session and the session after that,” he told the crowd. “We will not quit until you feel like we’ve done everything that we can do that you’ve asked us to do.” Burrows, R-Lubbock, encouraged officials to be forthright in their testimony. “We cannot come up with solutions, we cannot try to improve things, if we don’t know everything that you can share with us,” he said." body:
We’re taking back the U.S. Senate Majority.

---
| | | [ ](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/112583366/560957615/-1642061407?amount=15&refcode=20250801_em_fr_b1_np4c_sur_main&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNDIvMS84ODQ2NiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI4MjZjMzQ1Mi0wODZmLWYwMTEtOGRjOS02MDQ1YmRhOWQ5NmIiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=txa1ogJr2a-tC82amdRQMXqxkS0_MrlnQ__ZMK0nwuw=)

This is Roy Cooper, and I’m reaching out with exciting news that I wanted to make sure you heard from me:
[I’m officially running to be the next U.S. Senator from North Carolina.](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/112583368/560957617/-1642061407?amount=15&refcode=20250801_em_fr_b1_np4c_sur_main&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNDIvMS84ODQ2NiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI4MjZjMzQ1Mi0wODZmLWYwMTEtOGRjOS02MDQ1YmRhOWQ5NmIiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=txa1ogJr2a-tC82amdRQMXqxkS0_MrlnQ__ZMK0nwuw=)
We launched our campaign on Monday, and we’ve been overwhelmed by the response from Democratic supporters across the country. I never take running for office lightly, but more than ever, I _know_ that we can win together.
[_I’m grateful to have Speaker Emerita Nancy Pelosi's support in these critical first few days, and I need yours, too. Could you split a donation between their team and our campaign for Senate right now?_](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/112583370/560957619/-1642061407?amount=15&refcode=20250801_em_fr_b1_np4c_sur_main&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNDIvMS84ODQ2NiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI4MjZjMzQ1Mi0wODZmLWYwMTEtOGRjOS02MDQ1YmRhOWQ5NmIiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=txa1ogJr2a-tC82amdRQMXqxkS0_MrlnQ__ZMK0nwuw=)

name: Rob Bonta
subject: I’m suing Pam Bondi and Donald Trump
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY ROB BONTA FOR CALIFORNIA ATTORNEY GENERAL 2026 FPPC ID #145642]
body:
[ https://act.robbonta.com/go/4?refcode=em250801&t=1001&akid=1903%2E1088799%2Ex0nn5E ]Rob Bonta

Donald Trump and
his allies are trying to erase the existence of transgender individuals,
scare them into the shadows of society, and deny their
rights.

Trump has been relentlessly targeting the trans community from his first
day in office when he signed an Executive Order declaring the U.S. would
only recognize two sexes — followed by another that specifically targeted
trans kids.

It’s cruel, irresponsible, and unlawful.

[ https://act.robbonta.com/survey/em-250801?t=1002&akid=1903%2E1088799%2Ex0nn5E ]I’ve filed a lawsuit against Donald Trump, Pam Bondi, and the
administration challenging their discriminatory federal actions — and if
you’re with me, I hope you’ll add your name to my petition today.

ADD YOUR NAME

Trump and the DOJ are trying to insert themselves into private healthcare
decisions made between patients, providers, and families. They’re
demanding that medical providers discriminate against transgender
individuals — denying already vulnerable adolescents medically-necessary
care.

But let’s be clear: Trump and Bondi have no legal ground to stand on.
There is no federal law in this country prohibiting gender-affirming care.
In fact, California and many other states have laws protecting the right
to gender-affirming care.

All Americans, regardless of their gender identity, have the inalienable
right to equal protection under the law. That includes the right to access
healthcare free from discrimination, and to make important healthcare
decisions in consultation with their medical providers. Politicians aren’t
needed in our doctors’ offices.

Bottom line — this is for patients, parents, and providers to decide. Not
Donald Trump and Pam Bondi.

[ https://act.robbonta.com/survey/em-250801?t=1003&akid=1903%2E1088799%2Ex0nn5E ]If you agree Trump’s demeaning, inflammatory, and illegal attacks on
trans kids and the entire transgender community are dangerous and wrong,
please sign my petition.

While we’re witnessing a reprehensible rise in hate-fueled violence and
intimidation against our LGBTQ+ community, here in California, we will
continue to safeguard and protect the freedom of every individual.

In solidarity,
Rob

[ https://act.robbonta.com/go/1007?t=1004&akid=1903%2E1088799%2Ex0nn5E ][IMG]

Rob Bonta
Attorney General of California

This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com. Email is a critical way we communicate with grassroots supporters like you. However, if you would like to change your subscription preferences, here are some options:

Receive Fewer Emails: https://act.robbonta.com/signup/receive-fewer-emails/?t=1005&akid=1903%2E1088799%2Ex0nn5E
Update Your Information: https://act.robbonta.com/signup/update-your-information/
Unsubscribe: https://act.robbonta.com/unsubscribe/unsubscribe/?t=1006&akid=1903%2E1088799%2Ex0nn5E

Rob Bonta for Attorney General 2026
c/o Deane & Company
1700 Tribute Road, Suite 201
Sacramento, CA 95815
info@robbonta.com

[PAID FOR BY ROB BONTA FOR CALIFORNIA ATTORNEY GENERAL 2026 FPPC ID #145642]

Hi there, it's Jay. >From the bottom of my heart, thank you.
Because of you and countless other grassroots supporters, our campaign officially surpassed our major end-of-month fundraising goal, and we couldn't be more grateful. 🎉
Thanks to your continued support, our campaign to defeat Jason Miyares for Attorney General continues to grow each day. We’re traveling all over the Commonwealth, reaching more voters, training volunteers, and building our momentum.
But team, we still have a lot of work in front of us to win this race for Attorney General.
Donald Trump’s top campaign strategist recently held a major fundraiser for Jason Miyares in Richmond, and top MAGA donors are pouring money into this race.
They’re doing it for one reason: our momentum is scaring them and they want to stop this campaign.
You see the news from the Trump Administration, same as I do. The stakes of this election are too high for us to rest. So please, if you can, contribute $25 or more to help us keep our momentum going and fight back against Donald Trump and Jason Miyares. body:
A quick thank you from me (and maybe a Friday beer) |
#
---
I’ve got some great news, team: It’s International Beer Day AND we hit our July fundraising goal! I think this calls for a toast. 🍻 Before you log off for the weekend (and maybe grab a cold one yourself), I want to thank you for stepping up. **Because of your support, we’re charging into the August recess fired up and ready to hold Republicans accountable and train the next wave of leaders this fall.** Here’s to you:
**For fighting like hell.
For showing up when it counts.
And for giving me one more reason to raise this Oberon in celebration.** [](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/112583756/560959072/1700965107?email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&refcode=AU_FLHP_CL_GEN_TYE_AC_20250801_PM1_V1_S1&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNTkvMS8xMDY0NjIiLA0KICAiRGlzdHJpYnV0aW9uVW5pcXVlSWQiOiAiOTNlZWMzNGEtMTY2Zi1mMDExLThkYzktNjA0NWJkYTlkOTZiIiwNCiAgIkVtYWlsQWRkcmVzcyI6ICJkcHdpbGxpczY3QGdtYWlsLmNvbSINCn0%3D&hmac=GfetbPh5reqJbP-hMLOExhORRD3g0EwM3uoPoiVV4MI=) Enjoy your Friday — you’ve earned it. clean_body: A quick thank you from me (and maybe a Friday beer) | I’ve got some great news, team: It’s International Beer Day AND we hit our July fundraising goal! I think this calls for a toast. 🍻 Before you log off for the weekend (and maybe grab a cold one yourself), I want to thank you for stepping up. Because of your support, we’re charging into the August recess fired up and ready to hold Republicans accountable and train the next wave of leaders this fall. Here’s to you: For fighting like hell. For showing up when it counts. And for giving me one more reason to raise this Oberon in celebration. = Enjoy your Friday — you’ve earned it. name: Brad Finstad
subject: Why Won’t Walz Clean Up Our Voter Rolls?
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Finstad for Congress
body:
| | | | |
---
| Dear Friend, It’s happening again. Governor Walz and his liberal allies in Minnesota are standing in the way of basic election integrity. This week, the Minnesota Secretary of State's Office refused to share our voter registration list with President Trump’s Department of Justice as it works to identify non-citizens and clean up outdated or inaccurate records. Why? Because the left doesn’t want transparency. They don’t want accountability. And they certainly don’t want to admit that our system has serious vulnerabilities. Let’s be clear: this isn’t about suppressing votes. It’s about protecting the integrity of our elections. Every illegal or duplicate vote cancels out the voice of a law-abiding American citizen. That’s not democracy — that’s fraud. As your Congressman, I fully support President Trump’s efforts to secure our elections, clean up our voter rolls, and make sure only eligible citizens are voting. But we need leaders in Minnesota who actually believe in the rule of law. Unfortunately, under the Walz administration, that’s not what we’re getting. If you agree that we need secure, honest elections I’m asking for your help.
---
| | [Chip in here to help me fight the far-left and defend election integrity »](http://link.finstadforcongress.com/api/LinkHandler/getaction2?%3d)
---
| We’re not backing down. We’re going to keep fighting until Minnesota’s elections are fair, accurate, and worthy of the public’s trust. For Minnesota and for America, Brad

## Marine Vet Stops Walmart Stabbing Spree
Derrick Perry, 41, a Marine Corps veteran, is being praised nationwide after [confronting a knife-wielding attacker](http://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkL2upDAMhZ8mdBk5GRiYgmIburvtlsgQB7zKD0p8B_H2K_YVbmNZ-qRP55w_X_MXcuC0_cZIo9_8LD7Pic4aSITKLJwuwTVXweQWXK6YcyVMbrZgOw2DBjM75HDNbeNGT2Tp2dBo-q6FAbrh3eyjt9C_X09vkN4eXu1inSHjPSxAS0fQ8HjbYABj4fmyw8Ms5DoH1JsWjHl71QI5llwefzMn2cmHK3-oPNYcmzDuIkdVz1_KTspO6TpylRspO91iZSfo7_8-31Xf_ZSdsERyeqeS9VGQKzntc9FrTr7kJJw2HXndecOkTwwRi-iIteoquCw3rt_1oFU0J-0KRhRedd3z6fKZlJ2aMrrj5BC4vnrVwhaRw__I8uOtP6P9FwAA__8eUJcj) who wounded 11 people at a Walmart in Traverse City, Michigan, last weekend. [Cellphone video](http://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkDGu4yAUAE-Dux_BAwwUFLs_cpdIqVJaODwcshgsQ5L17VfZK_x2ihlprqfx5GKKeT67BW2Yw9hCGTO-a8LWcBtbzHtzt1Kby35y076UUtFlPwIF-UX1F2WjdzHto-i8DYiAvEPLlBRUU6lNd7fCGwwCOWDw6oZMoVA9IjVGB62U7KL92KimDCjvQR_YhF56iooJypgJRFD0sZXt8CgxtzuGtJcXbodbWbpk762tlfBfBAYCw98PJTBMuXisfwgMtbn2rAQGZoQBLaUR0lBOAXhP-FAJP4qeQN8IPz7XS34E8X3GNf1mV_aE78t57jbr13dMKdZeEUHnxcX0v95-vO1l4V8AAAD__5R8fsA) shows Perry, armed with a handgun, shouting commands at the 42-year-old suspect, Bradford Gille, and helping other shoppers pin him down until police arrived. Family members called it a proud moment, with Perry’s daughter saying he acted instinctively to protect others. Perry’s quick, disciplined response likely prevented further injuries in what police are calling a random and violent rampage. ## Tiny Taco Stand Earns Michelin Star
A tiny Mexico City taco stand has [made culinary history](http://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0LHSnSAQBeCn0W4dQK96C4o0dn_alA7Iopsg-w8wEt8-432F9GfOnO_8-lq_DAWK-09zova7X4vnNWLNAUvBtBaKdzEb52Kis8beJ3NGE92qhHqBmEHI1RkK9zq0TntEhX2LWk6vQcziNb_bQ_dv5YUdjXV2mtAqlH7YeosG59H1amtJP21iFlKJflRzJy26lxM4yUFI-fbNINBR4dT9ZorlQB9uvjB1G59t0Ecp37npfzRqadRSa-12ZvcwIpbK6U_HaW_U4inlAif-pc1EeFzwgUFhwAsTJNyQLgQDJ20HBopPIEE-uGY4uELAnIEyZAb2BSOcnLBRS5u0-64UAuVxagaxn4bCZ175718vrf4FAAD__44Okvw) as the first of its kind to earn a Michelin star. Operating since 1968 with just four beef tacos on the menu, Taquería El Califa de León impressed inspectors with its stripped down recipe: beef, salt, lime, and a fresh tortilla—sauce optional. Chef Arturo Rivera Martínez has run the 680-degree griddle for over two decades, serving the same no-frills tacos in a 100-square-foot space with no seats and barely room to stand. At nearly $5 a taco, it’s not cheap by local standards—but loyal customers say it’s worth every peso. As Rivera Martínez put it, _"[It’s cool](http://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0DvOpDAQBODTQGZkm5cJHGxCNptuiBq7PfTKjxFuDcvtVzNX-PNSqb7689geQJHy8zcktOEZNg5ly3jViMx4bkz5ZnClMmS_w36nUipC9puWehTSCKk2DxTvbWi9DYga-xatmsdBGjmapT3sgiOiCRKDCouHfkfZw2TMMI97cM61ZD9t0kilZT9p06kd_eglzmqQSi2hGSR64nJ2fwtlPjDEu7zx7FxJbbQH86s2_a9Gr41er-vqAlR2Jb0g359Mo9dFKT1Namj0GuisLBK5AyNlURlOkfAfOcjigxVfrcAoHEQKIDyKiCW3p_Wvi2KkOs3NIJ8JKH4n8I-_e1v9PwAA__-sS4ar),"_ and that’s about as flashy as it gets.
## Abandoned Baby Moose Finds New Home
A [baby moose named Cedar](http://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0MGunCAYBeCnwR0GEEZmwcLbxLRN7KpJl-Z3-FFahIkwY-jTN_YV7v7k5Hzn1zRP4IOP6w_Y0bjVzcWlOeKZA5aCx1x8rAUeKReIdoGl7illhGhnwYSiTFPGZws-1Fk21jhEgV2DhvdKMs2Uvjeb6RZwUqpO9NDrXijhWIf6xiXv751StvHmamOaccG6m9AtX9Aqy7DnknF-d0QytL6ko_2dfCwbulDTG4_2kfYmmK2UZybdQMRIxHieZ5tfMdb8p_69EkSMa0qWXi4iRqWlIGIcFog2RbR0iJZ-BB8t_bn5TD9gqXS6nDTT7-l1RKz0W6YThuLjSr8iHCXT4XGknOkXiGChOYx9nj4En289kWzdwYf_68qnb30b8S8AAP__ABOOJQ) was rescued in Ontario, Canada, after being found alone and disoriented. Just two months old and separated from his mother, Cedar had little chance of surviving in the wild. He was taken in by [Holly’s Haven Wildlife Rescue](http://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkM1unDAYRZ8GdkG2sQdYeMGkAaSUqGqUzHSF_PN5xlODB-yC6NNX01fI6m6ujo7OqR96YZ2dLm9iBG4uZojGDxNswUGMsAzRTnsUyocoJi2F3EfvA4hJDwQR9oTKJ4QHLazbB5pqbgAI5ClwXDCKSsTKKr1ymuNKCAkKi4NgWhZVpRRVoBADbahOLX_QUIkwQfmBlBmWoJlGUGCKMK5MQhFoG_2S3byd4hWM2_0KS6b8mDp-jfEekrxOSJOQZtu2zAgF0vvfj0NCmq47WaedNfATgvoDCWnuPsTwWCOtRuS1k7_mb333-Xz-y8I7iI9ybtuXTxmOLzWbb_Nz-92w8xL7j7e2qc5zXujyx2sdj-_rWh-1Sxeu75t1zoZDkVB0GYV1__3il8OunPwLAAD__-ghj3Q), where vets found he had limited vision in one eye. With nearby sanctuaries at capacity, the team quickly approved Cedar's move to [the Toronto Zoo](http://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0LGOhCAQBuCnwQ4zsCJSUFxjt9deaUYYVi4IGyVrvKe_uLk3uHKSyZ__-7_u0x1jivnxiSvZ8AhTDWXKdOyJaqVtqjGfFV3ZK2Y_43yupeyE2U8SpOIwcBCTx5jOqWu8DUSSbg1ZoVUHA6jBNIv12kNws9GoewO-JyXQoDOAQkqnuibaKw0GEBJuvRxaMZNXHkiLDoQwgXVAPtaytd8l5rpQSGd50da6sjbJLrU-d3b7YHJkcjyOo3Wzax0yOV4WJkeHGf1117KVXAuT45xi9vwy8TeKO_K48UwHX8pK_O-T_5TCRauV6bRRzWb984gpxb3XrIPHijG9W9R_z_ey8jcAAP__D2WGCA). Staff there are now prepping his new habitat and coordinating care with Holly’s Haven. Though he can't return to the wild, Cedar will serve as an educational ambassador, helping people connect with native wildlife up close.
